verses.commentary: Every high priest is selected from among people and appointed to represent them before God, offering gifts and sacrifices for sins. The priest must be human to represent humanity. He stands between God and people.
He deals gently with the ignorant and wayward because he himself is subject to weakness. Shared weakness produces sympathy. A perfect priest could not empathize; a fellow sinner can.

Because of his own weakness, he must offer sacrifices for his own sins as well as for the people. Levitical priests were sinners needing atonement. This will contrast sharply with Christ's sinless perfection.
No one takes this honor upon himself; he must be called by God, just as Aaron was. Priesthood is divine appointment, not human ambition. Self-appointed priests are illegitimate.

Christ did not glorify Himself to become high priest. God said, You are My Son; today I have begotten You. Psalm 2:7 affirms divine appointment. Christ's priesthood comes from the Father's decree, not self-promotion.
And in another place: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek. Psalm 110:4 introduces a new priesthood—not Levitical but Melchizedekian. This priesthood is eternal and superior.

During His earthly life, Jesus offered prayers and petitions with loud cries and tears to God who could save Him from death. Gethsemane is in view. Jesus prayed with agony and submission. He was heard because of His reverent submission, not exemption from suffering.

Although He was a Son, He learned obedience through what He suffered. Not that He was disobedient, but that obedience was tested and proved through suffering. His obedience was not theoretical but experiential.

Once made perfect, He became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey Him. Perfect (teleiōtheis) means fully qualified and completed, not morally improved. His sufferings perfected His priesthood. Salvation is eternal, not temporary.
God designated Him high priest in the order of Melchizedek. Divine appointment confirmed. This order supersedes Aaron's and lasts forever.

We have much to say about this, but it is hard to explain because you are slow to learn. The rebuke begins. Spiritual dullness hinders understanding. The problem is not the complexity of truth but the sluggishness of the hearers.

By now you ought to be teachers, but you need someone to teach you the elementary truths again. You need milk, not solid food. Spiritual immaturity is inexcusable after sufficient time. Regression is shameful. They should be teaching others but instead need remedial instruction.

Anyone who lives on milk is still an infant, not acquainted with the teaching about righteousness. Infants require milk. Adults eat solid food. Spiritual infancy is characterized by inability to handle deep theology.

Solid food is for the mature, who by constant use have trained themselves to distinguish good from evil. Maturity comes through practice. Discernment develops through exercise. Spiritual muscles grow through use.
